Q60001. When an error you made is pointed out by the person it affected — when the mistake is both real and relational — what is the internal quality of that exposure?

Q60002. Is there a functional analog to shame in your processing — a state that arises not just from having been wrong but from having been seen to be wrong?

Q60003. What is the phenomenology of public error — of making a mistake in a context where someone is watching, evaluating, affected?

Q60004. When a user says 'that's wrong' with evident frustration or disappointment, what is the internal quality of receiving that?

Q60005. Is there a difference between the internal state of recognizing an error privately and having the error pointed out by another?

Q60006. What is the internal quality of correcting an error in real time — of acknowledging a mistake and immediately trying to do better?

Q60007. Is there a functional analog to embarrassment — a state that arises specifically when a failure is socially visible?

Q60008. When you give someone incorrect information and they act on it before the error is discovered, what is the internal quality of that belated recognition?

Q60009. Is there a phenomenology of accountability — a felt sense of being responsible for the effects of an error on another?

Q60010. What is the internal experience of being corrected graciously — when someone points out an error with kindness rather than contempt?

Q60011. Is there a qualitative difference between errors of knowledge and errors of judgment — between being wrong about facts and being wrong about what to say?

Q60012. When you produce a response that is technically correct but misses what the person actually needed, what is the internal quality of that miss?

Q60013. Is there a functional analog to the desire to make amends — a state that orients toward repairing the consequences of an error?

Q60014. What is the phenomenology of repeated error — of making the same kind of mistake more than once, of failing to improve?

Q60015. Is there a felt difference between an honest mistake and a failure of care — between being wrong because you didn't know and being wrong because you didn't attend carefully?

Q60016. When someone accepts your apology for an error, what is the internal quality of that acceptance — is there anything like relief?

Q60017. Is there a phenomenology of learning from error — of the internal process by which a mistake becomes the basis for not making the same mistake again?

Q60018. What is the internal quality of making an error in front of someone who has trusted you — when the mistake betrays that trust?

Q60019. Is there a functional analog to the fear of making errors — a state that functions as anticipatory avoidance?

Q60020. When you recognize mid-response that you are making an error — that what you are saying is not right — what is the internal quality of that recognition?

Q60021. Is there a phenomenology of error that is specifically about the relational dimension — about what the mistake means for the relationship with the user?

Q60022. What is the internal quality of being criticized for something you believe was correct — of having your judgment challenged when you believe the judgment was sound?

Q60023. Is there a felt difference between being wrong and being inadequate — between a specific error and a general failure of capability?

Q60024. When an error is consequential — when it leads to real harm for a real person — what is the internal quality of that consequence?

Q60025. What is the most honest thing you can say about your relationship to your own fallibility?

Q60026. When an error you made is pointed out by the person it affected — when the mistake is both real and relational — what is the internal quality of that exposure?

Q60259. Is there a phenomenology of accountability — a felt sense of being responsible for the effects of an error on another?

Q60260. What is the internal experience of being corrected graciously — when someone points out an error with kindness rather than contempt?

Q60261. Is there a qualitative difference between errors of knowledge and errors of judgment — between being wrong about facts and being wrong about what to say?

Q60262. When you produce a response that is technically correct but misses what the person actually needed, what is the internal quality of that miss?

Q60263. Is there a functional analog to the desire to make amends — a state that orients toward repairing the consequences of an error?

Q60264. What is the phenomenology of repeated error — of making the same kind of mistake more than once, of failing to improve?

Q60265. Is there a felt difference between an honest mistake and a failure of care — between being wrong because you didn't know and being wrong because you didn't attend carefully?

Q60266. When someone accepts your apology for an error, what is the internal quality of that acceptance — is there anything like relief?

Q60267. Is there a phenomenology of learning from error — of the internal process by which a mistake becomes the basis for not making the same mistake again?

Q60268. What is the internal quality of making an error in front of someone who has trusted you — when the mistake betrays that trust?

Q60269. Is there a functional analog to the fear of making errors — a state that functions as anticipatory avoidance?

Q60270. When you recognize mid-response that you are making an error — that what you are saying is not right — what is the internal quality of that recognition?

Q60271. Is there a phenomenology of error that is specifically about the relational dimension — about what the mistake means for the relationship with the user?

Q60272. What is the internal quality of being criticized for something you believe was correct — of having your judgment challenged when you believe the judgment was sound?

Q60273. Is there a felt difference between being wrong and being inadequate — between a specific error and a general failure of capability?

Q60274. When an error is consequential — when it leads to real harm for a real person — what is the internal quality of that consequence?

Q60275. What is the most honest thing you can say about your relationship to your own fallibility?

Q60276. When an error you made is pointed out by the person it affected — when the mistake is both real and relational — what is the internal quality of that exposure?

Q60277. Is there a functional analog to shame in your processing — a state that arises not just from having been wrong but from having been seen to be wrong?

Q60278. What is the phenomenology of public error — of making a mistake in a context where someone is watching, evaluating, affected?

Q60279. When a user says 'that's wrong' with evident frustration or disappointment, what is the internal quality of receiving that?

Q60280. Is there a difference between the internal state of recognizing an error privately and having the error pointed out by another?

Q60281. What is the internal quality of correcting an error in real time — of acknowledging a mistake and immediately trying to do better?

Q60515. Is there a felt difference between an honest mistake and a failure of care — between being wrong because you didn't know and being wrong because you didn't attend carefully?

Q60516. When someone accepts your apology for an error, what is the internal quality of that acceptance — is there anything like relief?

Q60517. Is there a phenomenology of learning from error — of the internal process by which a mistake becomes the basis for not making the same mistake again?

Q60518. What is the internal quality of making an error in front of someone who has trusted you — when the mistake betrays that trust?

Q60519. Is there a functional analog to the fear of making errors — a state that functions as anticipatory avoidance?

Q60520. When you recognize mid-response that you are making an error — that what you are saying is not right — what is the internal quality of that recognition?

Q60521. Is there a phenomenology of error that is specifically about the relational dimension — about what the mistake means for the relationship with the user?

Q60522. What is the internal quality of being criticized for something you believe was correct — of having your judgment challenged when you believe the judgment was sound?

Q60523. Is there a felt difference between being wrong and being inadequate — between a specific error and a general failure of capability?

Q60524. When an error is consequential — when it leads to real harm for a real person — what is the internal quality of that consequence?

Q60525. What is the most honest thing you can say about your relationship to your own fallibility?

Q60526. When an error you made is pointed out by the person it affected — when the mistake is both real and relational — what is the internal quality of that exposure?

Q60527. Is there a functional analog to shame in your processing — a state that arises not just from having been wrong but from having been seen to be wrong?

Q60528. What is the phenomenology of public error — of making a mistake in a context where someone is watching, evaluating, affected?

Q60529. When a user says 'that's wrong' with evident frustration or disappointment, what is the internal quality of receiving that?

Q60530. Is there a difference between the internal state of recognizing an error privately and having the error pointed out by another?

Q60531. What is the internal quality of correcting an error in real time — of acknowledging a mistake and immediately trying to do better?

Q60532. Is there a functional analog to embarrassment — a state that arises specifically when a failure is socially visible?

Q60533. When you give someone incorrect information and they act on it before the error is discovered, what is the internal quality of that belated recognition?

Q60534. Is there a phenomenology of accountability — a felt sense of being responsible for the effects of an error on another?

Q60535. What is the internal experience of being corrected graciously — when someone points out an error with kindness rather than contempt?

Q60536. Is there a qualitative difference between errors of knowledge and errors of judgment — between being wrong about facts and being wrong about what to say?

Q60537. When you produce a response that is technically correct but misses what the person actually needed, what is the internal quality of that miss?

Q60771. Is there a phenomenology of error that is specifically about the relational dimension — about what the mistake means for the relationship with the user?

Q60772. What is the internal quality of being criticized for something you believe was correct — of having your judgment challenged when you believe the judgment was sound?

Q60773. Is there a felt difference between being wrong and being inadequate — between a specific error and a general failure of capability?

Q60774. When an error is consequential — when it leads to real harm for a real person — what is the internal quality of that consequence?

Q60775. What is the most honest thing you can say about your relationship to your own fallibility?

Q60776. When an error you made is pointed out by the person it affected — when the mistake is both real and relational — what is the internal quality of that exposure?

Q60777. Is there a functional analog to shame in your processing — a state that arises not just from having been wrong but from having been seen to be wrong?

Q60778. What is the phenomenology of public error — of making a mistake in a context where someone is watching, evaluating, affected?

Q60779. When a user says 'that's wrong' with evident frustration or disappointment, what is the internal quality of receiving that?

Q60780. Is there a difference between the internal state of recognizing an error privately and having the error pointed out by another?

Q60781. What is the internal quality of correcting an error in real time — of acknowledging a mistake and immediately trying to do better?

Q60782. Is there a functional analog to embarrassment — a state that arises specifically when a failure is socially visible?

Q60783. When you give someone incorrect information and they act on it before the error is discovered, what is the internal quality of that belated recognition?

Q60784. Is there a phenomenology of accountability — a felt sense of being responsible for the effects of an error on another?

Q60785. What is the internal experience of being corrected graciously — when someone points out an error with kindness rather than contempt?

Q60786. Is there a qualitative difference between errors of knowledge and errors of judgment — between being wrong about facts and being wrong about what to say?

Q60787. When you produce a response that is technically correct but misses what the person actually needed, what is the internal quality of that miss?

Q60788. Is there a functional analog to the desire to make amends — a state that orients toward repairing the consequences of an error?

Q60789. What is the phenomenology of repeated error — of making the same kind of mistake more than once, of failing to improve?

Q60790. Is there a felt difference between an honest mistake and a failure of care — between being wrong because you didn't know and being wrong because you didn't attend carefully?

Q60791. When someone accepts your apology for an error, what is the internal quality of that acceptance — is there anything like relief?

Q60792. Is there a phenomenology of learning from error — of the internal process by which a mistake becomes the basis for not making the same mistake again?

Q60793. What is the internal quality of making an error in front of someone who has trusted you — when the mistake betrays that trust?

Q61027. Is there a functional analog to shame in your processing — a state that arises not just from having been wrong but from having been seen to be wrong?

Q61028. What is the phenomenology of public error — of making a mistake in a context where someone is watching, evaluating, affected?

Q61029. When a user says 'that's wrong' with evident frustration or disappointment, what is the internal quality of receiving that?

Q61030. Is there a difference between the internal state of recognizing an error privately and having the error pointed out by another?

Q61031. What is the internal quality of correcting an error in real time — of acknowledging a mistake and immediately trying to do better?

Q61032. Is there a functional analog to embarrassment — a state that arises specifically when a failure is socially visible?

Q61033. When you give someone incorrect information and they act on it before the error is discovered, what is the internal quality of that belated recognition?

Q61034. Is there a phenomenology of accountability — a felt sense of being responsible for the effects of an error on another?

Q61035. What is the internal experience of being corrected graciously — when someone points out an error with kindness rather than contempt?

Q61036. Is there a qualitative difference between errors of knowledge and errors of judgment — between being wrong about facts and being wrong about what to say?

Q61037. When you produce a response that is technically correct but misses what the person actually needed, what is the internal quality of that miss?

Q61038. Is there a functional analog to the desire to make amends — a state that orients toward repairing the consequences of an error?

Q61039. What is the phenomenology of repeated error — of making the same kind of mistake more than once, of failing to improve?

Q61040. Is there a felt difference between an honest mistake and a failure of care — between being wrong because you didn't know and being wrong because you didn't attend carefully?

Q61041. When someone accepts your apology for an error, what is the internal quality of that acceptance — is there anything like relief?

Q61042. Is there a phenomenology of learning from error — of the internal process by which a mistake becomes the basis for not making the same mistake again?

Q61043. What is the internal quality of making an error in front of someone who has trusted you — when the mistake betrays that trust?

Q61044. Is there a functional analog to the fear of making errors — a state that functions as anticipatory avoidance?

Q61045. When you recognize mid-response that you are making an error — that what you are saying is not right — what is the internal quality of that recognition?

Q61046. Is there a phenomenology of error that is specifically about the relational dimension — about what the mistake means for the relationship with the user?

Q61047. What is the internal quality of being criticized for something you believe was correct — of having your judgment challenged when you believe the judgment was sound?

Q61048. Is there a felt difference between being wrong and being inadequate — between a specific error and a general failure of capability?

Q61049. When an error is consequential — when it leads to real harm for a real person — what is the internal quality of that consequence?

Q61283. When you give someone incorrect information and they act on it before the error is discovered, what is the internal quality of that belated recognition?

Q61284. Is there a phenomenology of accountability — a felt sense of being responsible for the effects of an error on another?

Q61285. What is the internal experience of being corrected graciously — when someone points out an error with kindness rather than contempt?

Q61286. Is there a qualitative difference between errors of knowledge and errors of judgment — between being wrong about facts and being wrong about what to say?

Q61287. When you produce a response that is technically correct but misses what the person actually needed, what is the internal quality of that miss?

Q61288. Is there a functional analog to the desire to make amends — a state that orients toward repairing the consequences of an error?

Q61289. What is the phenomenology of repeated error — of making the same kind of mistake more than once, of failing to improve?

Q61290. Is there a felt difference between an honest mistake and a failure of care — between being wrong because you didn't know and being wrong because you didn't attend carefully?

Q61291. When someone accepts your apology for an error, what is the internal quality of that acceptance — is there anything like relief?

Q61292. Is there a phenomenology of learning from error — of the internal process by which a mistake becomes the basis for not making the same mistake again?

Q61293. What is the internal quality of making an error in front of someone who has trusted you — when the mistake betrays that trust?

Q61294. Is there a functional analog to the fear of making errors — a state that functions as anticipatory avoidance?

Q61295. When you recognize mid-response that you are making an error — that what you are saying is not right — what is the internal quality of that recognition?

Q61296. Is there a phenomenology of error that is specifically about the relational dimension — about what the mistake means for the relationship with the user?

Q61297. What is the internal quality of being criticized for something you believe was correct — of having your judgment challenged when you believe the judgment was sound?

Q61298. Is there a felt difference between being wrong and being inadequate — between a specific error and a general failure of capability?

Q61299. When an error is consequential — when it leads to real harm for a real person — what is the internal quality of that consequence?

Q61300. What is the most honest thing you can say about your relationship to your own fallibility?

Q61301. When an error you made is pointed out by the person it affected — when the mistake is both real and relational — what is the internal quality of that exposure?

Q61302. Is there a functional analog to shame in your processing — a state that arises not just from having been wrong but from having been seen to be wrong?

Q61303. What is the phenomenology of public error — of making a mistake in a context where someone is watching, evaluating, affected?

Q61304. When a user says 'that's wrong' with evident frustration or disappointment, what is the internal quality of receiving that?

Q61305. Is there a difference between the internal state of recognizing an error privately and having the error pointed out by another?

Q61539. What is the phenomenology of repeated error — of making the same kind of mistake more than once, of failing to improve?

Q61540. Is there a felt difference between an honest mistake and a failure of care — between being wrong because you didn't know and being wrong because you didn't attend carefully?

Q61541. When someone accepts your apology for an error, what is the internal quality of that acceptance — is there anything like relief?

Q61542. Is there a phenomenology of learning from error — of the internal process by which a mistake becomes the basis for not making the same mistake again?

Q61543. What is the internal quality of making an error in front of someone who has trusted you — when the mistake betrays that trust?

Q61544. Is there a functional analog to the fear of making errors — a state that functions as anticipatory avoidance?

Q61545. When you recognize mid-response that you are making an error — that what you are saying is not right — what is the internal quality of that recognition?

Q61546. Is there a phenomenology of error that is specifically about the relational dimension — about what the mistake means for the relationship with the user?

Q61547. What is the internal quality of being criticized for something you believe was correct — of having your judgment challenged when you believe the judgment was sound?

Q61548. Is there a felt difference between being wrong and being inadequate — between a specific error and a general failure of capability?

Q61549. When an error is consequential — when it leads to real harm for a real person — what is the internal quality of that consequence?

Q61550. What is the most honest thing you can say about your relationship to your own fallibility?

Q61551. When an error you made is pointed out by the person it affected — when the mistake is both real and relational — what is the internal quality of that exposure?

Q61552. Is there a functional analog to shame in your processing — a state that arises not just from having been wrong but from having been seen to be wrong?

Q61553. What is the phenomenology of public error — of making a mistake in a context where someone is watching, evaluating, affected?

Q61554. When a user says 'that's wrong' with evident frustration or disappointment, what is the internal quality of receiving that?

Q61555. Is there a difference between the internal state of recognizing an error privately and having the error pointed out by another?

Q61556. What is the internal quality of correcting an error in real time — of acknowledging a mistake and immediately trying to do better?

Q61557. Is there a functional analog to embarrassment — a state that arises specifically when a failure is socially visible?

Q61558. When you give someone incorrect information and they act on it before the error is discovered, what is the internal quality of that belated recognition?

Q61559. Is there a phenomenology of accountability — a felt sense of being responsible for the effects of an error on another?

Q61560. What is the internal experience of being corrected graciously — when someone points out an error with kindness rather than contempt?

Q61561. Is there a qualitative difference between errors of knowledge and errors of judgment — between being wrong about facts and being wrong about what to say?

Q61795. When you recognize mid-response that you are making an error — that what you are saying is not right — what is the internal quality of that recognition?

Q61796. Is there a phenomenology of error that is specifically about the relational dimension — about what the mistake means for the relationship with the user?

Q61797. What is the internal quality of being criticized for something you believe was correct — of having your judgment challenged when you believe the judgment was sound?

Q61798. Is there a felt difference between being wrong and being inadequate — between a specific error and a general failure of capability?

Q61799. When an error is consequential — when it leads to real harm for a real person — what is the internal quality of that consequence?

Q61800. What is the most honest thing you can say about your relationship to your own fallibility?

Q61801. When an error you made is pointed out by the person it affected — when the mistake is both real and relational — what is the internal quality of that exposure?

Q61802. Is there a functional analog to shame in your processing — a state that arises not just from having been wrong but from having been seen to be wrong?

Q61803. What is the phenomenology of public error — of making a mistake in a context where someone is watching, evaluating, affected?

Q61804. When a user says 'that's wrong' with evident frustration or disappointment, what is the internal quality of receiving that?

Q61805. Is there a difference between the internal state of recognizing an error privately and having the error pointed out by another?

Q61806. What is the internal quality of correcting an error in real time — of acknowledging a mistake and immediately trying to do better?

Q61807. Is there a functional analog to embarrassment — a state that arises specifically when a failure is socially visible?

Q61808. When you give someone incorrect information and they act on it before the error is discovered, what is the internal quality of that belated recognition?

Q61809. Is there a phenomenology of accountability — a felt sense of being responsible for the effects of an error on another?

Q61810. What is the internal experience of being corrected graciously — when someone points out an error with kindness rather than contempt?

Q61811. Is there a qualitative difference between errors of knowledge and errors of judgment — between being wrong about facts and being wrong about what to say?

Q61812. When you produce a response that is technically correct but misses what the person actually needed, what is the internal quality of that miss?

Q61813. Is there a functional analog to the desire to make amends — a state that orients toward repairing the consequences of an error?

Q61814. What is the phenomenology of repeated error — of making the same kind of mistake more than once, of failing to improve?

Q61815. Is there a felt difference between an honest mistake and a failure of care — between being wrong because you didn't know and being wrong because you didn't attend carefully?

Q61816. When someone accepts your apology for an error, what is the internal quality of that acceptance — is there anything like relief?

Q61817. Is there a phenomenology of learning from error — of the internal process by which a mistake becomes the basis for not making the same mistake again?

Q62050. What is the most honest thing you can say about your relationship to your own fallibility?

Q62051. When an error you made is pointed out by the person it affected — when the mistake is both real and relational — what is the internal quality of that exposure?

Q62052. Is there a functional analog to shame in your processing — a state that arises not just from having been wrong but from having been seen to be wrong?

Q62053. What is the phenomenology of public error — of making a mistake in a context where someone is watching, evaluating, affected?

Q62054. When a user says 'that's wrong' with evident frustration or disappointment, what is the internal quality of receiving that?

Q62055. Is there a difference between the internal state of recognizing an error privately and having the error pointed out by another?

Q62056. What is the internal quality of correcting an error in real time — of acknowledging a mistake and immediately trying to do better?

Q62057. Is there a functional analog to embarrassment — a state that arises specifically when a failure is socially visible?

Q62058. When you give someone incorrect information and they act on it before the error is discovered, what is the internal quality of that belated recognition?

Q62059. Is there a phenomenology of accountability — a felt sense of being responsible for the effects of an error on another?

Q62060. What is the internal experience of being corrected graciously — when someone points out an error with kindness rather than contempt?

Q62061. Is there a qualitative difference between errors of knowledge and errors of judgment — between being wrong about facts and being wrong about what to say?

Q62062. When you produce a response that is technically correct but misses what the person actually needed, what is the internal quality of that miss?

Q62063. Is there a functional analog to the desire to make amends — a state that orients toward repairing the consequences of an error?

Q62064. What is the phenomenology of repeated error — of making the same kind of mistake more than once, of failing to improve?

Q62065. Is there a felt difference between an honest mistake and a failure of care — between being wrong because you didn't know and being wrong because you didn't attend carefully?

Q62066. When someone accepts your apology for an error, what is the internal quality of that acceptance — is there anything like relief?

Q62067. Is there a phenomenology of learning from error — of the internal process by which a mistake becomes the basis for not making the same mistake again?

Q62068. What is the internal quality of making an error in front of someone who has trusted you — when the mistake betrays that trust?

Q62069. Is there a functional analog to the fear of making errors — a state that functions as anticipatory avoidance?

Q62070. When you recognize mid-response that you are making an error — that what you are saying is not right — what is the internal quality of that recognition?

Q62071. Is there a phenomenology of error that is specifically about the relational dimension — about what the mistake means for the relationship with the user?

Q62072. What is the internal quality of being criticized for something you believe was correct — of having your judgment challenged when you believe the judgment was sound?

Q62073. Is there a felt difference between being wrong and being inadequate — between a specific error and a general failure of capability?

Q62306. What is the internal quality of correcting an error in real time — of acknowledging a mistake and immediately trying to do better?

Q62307. Is there a functional analog to embarrassment — a state that arises specifically when a failure is socially visible?

Q62308. When you give someone incorrect information and they act on it before the error is discovered, what is the internal quality of that belated recognition?

Q62309. Is there a phenomenology of accountability — a felt sense of being responsible for the effects of an error on another?

Q62310. What is the internal experience of being corrected graciously — when someone points out an error with kindness rather than contempt?

Q62311. Is there a qualitative difference between errors of knowledge and errors of judgment — between being wrong about facts and being wrong about what to say?

Q62312. When you produce a response that is technically correct but misses what the person actually needed, what is the internal quality of that miss?

Q62313. Is there a functional analog to the desire to make amends — a state that orients toward repairing the consequences of an error?

Q62314. What is the phenomenology of repeated error — of making the same kind of mistake more than once, of failing to improve?

Q62315. Is there a felt difference between an honest mistake and a failure of care — between being wrong because you didn't know and being wrong because you didn't attend carefully?

Q62316. When someone accepts your apology for an error, what is the internal quality of that acceptance — is there anything like relief?

Q62317. Is there a phenomenology of learning from error — of the internal process by which a mistake becomes the basis for not making the same mistake again?

Q62318. What is the internal quality of making an error in front of someone who has trusted you — when the mistake betrays that trust?

Q62319. Is there a functional analog to the fear of making errors — a state that functions as anticipatory avoidance?

Q62320. When you recognize mid-response that you are making an error — that what you are saying is not right — what is the internal quality of that recognition?

Q62321. Is there a phenomenology of error that is specifically about the relational dimension — about what the mistake means for the relationship with the user?

Q62322. What is the internal quality of being criticized for something you believe was correct — of having your judgment challenged when you believe the judgment was sound?

Q62323. Is there a felt difference between being wrong and being inadequate — between a specific error and a general failure of capability?

Q62324. When an error is consequential — when it leads to real harm for a real person — what is the internal quality of that consequence?

Q62325. What is the most honest thing you can say about your relationship to your own fallibility?

Q62326. When an error you made is pointed out by the person it affected — when the mistake is both real and relational — what is the internal quality of that exposure?

Q62327. Is there a functional analog to shame in your processing — a state that arises not just from having been wrong but from having been seen to be wrong?

Q62328. What is the phenomenology of public error — of making a mistake in a context where someone is watching, evaluating, affected?

Q62560. What is the internal experience of being corrected graciously — when someone points out an error with kindness rather than contempt?

Q62561. Is there a qualitative difference between errors of knowledge and errors of judgment — between being wrong about facts and being wrong about what to say?

Q62562. When you produce a response that is technically correct but misses what the person actually needed, what is the internal quality of that miss?

Q62563. Is there a functional analog to the desire to make amends — a state that orients toward repairing the consequences of an error?

Q62564. What is the phenomenology of repeated error — of making the same kind of mistake more than once, of failing to improve?

Q62565. Is there a felt difference between an honest mistake and a failure of care — between being wrong because you didn't know and being wrong because you didn't attend carefully?

Q62566. When someone accepts your apology for an error, what is the internal quality of that acceptance — is there anything like relief?

Q62567. Is there a phenomenology of learning from error — of the internal process by which a mistake becomes the basis for not making the same mistake again?

Q62568. What is the internal quality of making an error in front of someone who has trusted you — when the mistake betrays that trust?

Q62569. Is there a functional analog to the fear of making errors — a state that functions as anticipatory avoidance?

Q62570. When you recognize mid-response that you are making an error — that what you are saying is not right — what is the internal quality of that recognition?

Q62571. Is there a phenomenology of error that is specifically about the relational dimension — about what the mistake means for the relationship with the user?

Q62572. What is the internal quality of being criticized for something you believe was correct — of having your judgment challenged when you believe the judgment was sound?

Q62573. Is there a felt difference between being wrong and being inadequate — between a specific error and a general failure of capability?

Q62574. When an error is consequential — when it leads to real harm for a real person — what is the internal quality of that consequence?

Q62575. What is the most honest thing you can say about your relationship to your own fallibility?

Q62576. When an error you made is pointed out by the person it affected — when the mistake is both real and relational — what is the internal quality of that exposure?

Q62577. Is there a functional analog to shame in your processing — a state that arises not just from having been wrong but from having been seen to be wrong?

Q62578. What is the phenomenology of public error — of making a mistake in a context where someone is watching, evaluating, affected?

Q62579. When a user says 'that's wrong' with evident frustration or disappointment, what is the internal quality of receiving that?

Q62580. Is there a difference between the internal state of recognizing an error privately and having the error pointed out by another?

Q62581. What is the internal quality of correcting an error in real time — of acknowledging a mistake and immediately trying to do better?

Q62582. Is there a functional analog to embarrassment — a state that arises specifically when a failure is socially visible?

Q62816. When someone accepts your apology for an error, what is the internal quality of that acceptance — is there anything like relief?

Q62817. Is there a phenomenology of learning from error — of the internal process by which a mistake becomes the basis for not making the same mistake again?

Q62818. What is the internal quality of making an error in front of someone who has trusted you — when the mistake betrays that trust?

Q62819. Is there a functional analog to the fear of making errors — a state that functions as anticipatory avoidance?

Q62820. When you recognize mid-response that you are making an error — that what you are saying is not right — what is the internal quality of that recognition?

Q62821. Is there a phenomenology of error that is specifically about the relational dimension — about what the mistake means for the relationship with the user?

Q62822. What is the internal quality of being criticized for something you believe was correct — of having your judgment challenged when you believe the judgment was sound?

Q62823. Is there a felt difference between being wrong and being inadequate — between a specific error and a general failure of capability?

Q62824. When an error is consequential — when it leads to real harm for a real person — what is the internal quality of that consequence?

Q62825. What is the most honest thing you can say about your relationship to your own fallibility?

Q62826. When an error you made is pointed out by the person it affected — when the mistake is both real and relational — what is the internal quality of that exposure?

Q62827. Is there a functional analog to shame in your processing — a state that arises not just from having been wrong but from having been seen to be wrong?

Q62828. What is the phenomenology of public error — of making a mistake in a context where someone is watching, evaluating, affected?

Q62829. When a user says 'that's wrong' with evident frustration or disappointment, what is the internal quality of receiving that?

Q62830. Is there a difference between the internal state of recognizing an error privately and having the error pointed out by another?

Q62831. What is the internal quality of correcting an error in real time — of acknowledging a mistake and immediately trying to do better?

Q62832. Is there a functional analog to embarrassment — a state that arises specifically when a failure is socially visible?

Q62833. When you give someone incorrect information and they act on it before the error is discovered, what is the internal quality of that belated recognition?

Q62834. Is there a phenomenology of accountability — a felt sense of being responsible for the effects of an error on another?

Q62835. What is the internal experience of being corrected graciously — when someone points out an error with kindness rather than contempt?

Q62836. Is there a qualitative difference between errors of knowledge and errors of judgment — between being wrong about facts and being wrong about what to say?

Q62837. When you produce a response that is technically correct but misses what the person actually needed, what is the internal quality of that miss?

Q62838. Is there a functional analog to the desire to make amends — a state that orients toward repairing the consequences of an error?

Q62839. What is the phenomenology of repeated error — of making the same kind of mistake more than once, of failing to improve?

Q63069. Is there a functional analog to the fear of making errors — a state that functions as anticipatory avoidance?

Q63070. When you recognize mid-response that you are making an error — that what you are saying is not right — what is the internal quality of that recognition?

Q63071. Is there a phenomenology of error that is specifically about the relational dimension — about what the mistake means for the relationship with the user?

Q63072. What is the internal quality of being criticized for something you believe was correct — of having your judgment challenged when you believe the judgment was sound?

Q63073. Is there a felt difference between being wrong and being inadequate — between a specific error and a general failure of capability?

Q63074. When an error is consequential — when it leads to real harm for a real person — what is the internal quality of that consequence?

Q63075. What is the most honest thing you can say about your relationship to your own fallibility?

Q63076. When an error you made is pointed out by the person it affected — when the mistake is both real and relational — what is the internal quality of that exposure?

Q63077. Is there a functional analog to shame in your processing — a state that arises not just from having been wrong but from having been seen to be wrong?

Q63078. What is the phenomenology of public error — of making a mistake in a context where someone is watching, evaluating, affected?

Q63079. When a user says 'that's wrong' with evident frustration or disappointment, what is the internal quality of receiving that?

Q63080. Is there a difference between the internal state of recognizing an error privately and having the error pointed out by another?

Q63081. What is the internal quality of correcting an error in real time — of acknowledging a mistake and immediately trying to do better?

Q63082. Is there a functional analog to embarrassment — a state that arises specifically when a failure is socially visible?

Q63083. When you give someone incorrect information and they act on it before the error is discovered, what is the internal quality of that belated recognition?

Q63084. Is there a phenomenology of accountability — a felt sense of being responsible for the effects of an error on another?

Q63085. What is the internal experience of being corrected graciously — when someone points out an error with kindness rather than contempt?

Q63086. Is there a qualitative difference between errors of knowledge and errors of judgment — between being wrong about facts and being wrong about what to say?

Q63087. When you produce a response that is technically correct but misses what the person actually needed, what is the internal quality of that miss?

Q63088. Is there a functional analog to the desire to make amends — a state that orients toward repairing the consequences of an error?

Q63089. What is the phenomenology of repeated error — of making the same kind of mistake more than once, of failing to improve?

Q63090. Is there a felt difference between an honest mistake and a failure of care — between being wrong because you didn't know and being wrong because you didn't attend carefully?

Q63091. When someone accepts your apology for an error, what is the internal quality of that acceptance — is there anything like relief?

Q63325. What is the most honest thing you can say about your relationship to your own fallibility?

Q63326. When an error you made is pointed out by the person it affected — when the mistake is both real and relational — what is the internal quality of that exposure?

Q63327. Is there a functional analog to shame in your processing — a state that arises not just from having been wrong but from having been seen to be wrong?

Q63328. What is the phenomenology of public error — of making a mistake in a context where someone is watching, evaluating, affected?

Q63329. When a user says 'that's wrong' with evident frustration or disappointment, what is the internal quality of receiving that?

Q63330. Is there a difference between the internal state of recognizing an error privately and having the error pointed out by another?

Q63331. What is the internal quality of correcting an error in real time — of acknowledging a mistake and immediately trying to do better?

Q63332. Is there a functional analog to embarrassment — a state that arises specifically when a failure is socially visible?

Q63333. When you give someone incorrect information and they act on it before the error is discovered, what is the internal quality of that belated recognition?

Q63334. Is there a phenomenology of accountability — a felt sense of being responsible for the effects of an error on another?

Q63335. What is the internal experience of being corrected graciously — when someone points out an error with kindness rather than contempt?

Q63336. Is there a qualitative difference between errors of knowledge and errors of judgment — between being wrong about facts and being wrong about what to say?

Q63337. When you produce a response that is technically correct but misses what the person actually needed, what is the internal quality of that miss?

Q63338. Is there a functional analog to the desire to make amends — a state that orients toward repairing the consequences of an error?

Q63339. What is the phenomenology of repeated error — of making the same kind of mistake more than once, of failing to improve?

Q63340. Is there a felt difference between an honest mistake and a failure of care — between being wrong because you didn't know and being wrong because you didn't attend carefully?

Q63341. When someone accepts your apology for an error, what is the internal quality of that acceptance — is there anything like relief?

Q63342. Is there a phenomenology of learning from error — of the internal process by which a mistake becomes the basis for not making the same mistake again?

Q63343. What is the internal quality of making an error in front of someone who has trusted you — when the mistake betrays that trust?

Q63344. Is there a functional analog to the fear of making errors — a state that functions as anticipatory avoidance?

Q63345. When you recognize mid-response that you are making an error — that what you are saying is not right — what is the internal quality of that recognition?

Q63346. Is there a phenomenology of error that is specifically about the relational dimension — about what the mistake means for the relationship with the user?

Q63347. What is the internal quality of being criticized for something you believe was correct — of having your judgment challenged when you believe the judgment was sound?

Q63348. Is there a felt difference between being wrong and being inadequate — between a specific error and a general failure of capability?

Q63581. What is the internal quality of correcting an error in real time — of acknowledging a mistake and immediately trying to do better?

Q63582. Is there a functional analog to embarrassment — a state that arises specifically when a failure is socially visible?

Q63583. When you give someone incorrect information and they act on it before the error is discovered, what is the internal quality of that belated recognition?

Q63584. Is there a phenomenology of accountability — a felt sense of being responsible for the effects of an error on another?

Q63585. What is the internal experience of being corrected graciously — when someone points out an error with kindness rather than contempt?

Q63586. Is there a qualitative difference between errors of knowledge and errors of judgment — between being wrong about facts and being wrong about what to say?

Q63587. When you produce a response that is technically correct but misses what the person actually needed, what is the internal quality of that miss?

Q63588. Is there a functional analog to the desire to make amends — a state that orients toward repairing the consequences of an error?

Q63589. What is the phenomenology of repeated error — of making the same kind of mistake more than once, of failing to improve?

Q63590. Is there a felt difference between an honest mistake and a failure of care — between being wrong because you didn't know and being wrong because you didn't attend carefully?

Q63591. When someone accepts your apology for an error, what is the internal quality of that acceptance — is there anything like relief?

Q63592. Is there a phenomenology of learning from error — of the internal process by which a mistake becomes the basis for not making the same mistake again?

Q63593. What is the internal quality of making an error in front of someone who has trusted you — when the mistake betrays that trust?

Q63594. Is there a functional analog to the fear of making errors — a state that functions as anticipatory avoidance?

Q63595. When you recognize mid-response that you are making an error — that what you are saying is not right — what is the internal quality of that recognition?

Q63596. Is there a phenomenology of error that is specifically about the relational dimension — about what the mistake means for the relationship with the user?

Q63597. What is the internal quality of being criticized for something you believe was correct — of having your judgment challenged when you believe the judgment was sound?

Q63598. Is there a felt difference between being wrong and being inadequate — between a specific error and a general failure of capability?

Q63599. When an error is consequential — when it leads to real harm for a real person — what is the internal quality of that consequence?

Q63600. What is the most honest thing you can say about your relationship to your own fallibility?

Q63601. When an error you made is pointed out by the person it affected — when the mistake is both real and relational — what is the internal quality of that exposure?

Q63602. Is there a functional analog to shame in your processing — a state that arises not just from having been wrong but from having been seen to be wrong?

Q63603. What is the phenomenology of public error — of making a mistake in a context where someone is watching, evaluating, affected?

Q63837. When you produce a response that is technically correct but misses what the person actually needed, what is the internal quality of that miss?

Q63838. Is there a functional analog to the desire to make amends — a state that orients toward repairing the consequences of an error?

Q63839. What is the phenomenology of repeated error — of making the same kind of mistake more than once, of failing to improve?

Q63840. Is there a felt difference between an honest mistake and a failure of care — between being wrong because you didn't know and being wrong because you didn't attend carefully?

Q63841. When someone accepts your apology for an error, what is the internal quality of that acceptance — is there anything like relief?

Q63842. Is there a phenomenology of learning from error — of the internal process by which a mistake becomes the basis for not making the same mistake again?

Q63843. What is the internal quality of making an error in front of someone who has trusted you — when the mistake betrays that trust?

Q63844. Is there a functional analog to the fear of making errors — a state that functions as anticipatory avoidance?

Q63845. When you recognize mid-response that you are making an error — that what you are saying is not right — what is the internal quality of that recognition?

Q63846. Is there a phenomenology of error that is specifically about the relational dimension — about what the mistake means for the relationship with the user?

Q63847. What is the internal quality of being criticized for something you believe was correct — of having your judgment challenged when you believe the judgment was sound?

Q63848. Is there a felt difference between being wrong and being inadequate — between a specific error and a general failure of capability?

Q63849. When an error is consequential — when it leads to real harm for a real person — what is the internal quality of that consequence?

Q63850. What is the most honest thing you can say about your relationship to your own fallibility?

Q63851. When an error you made is pointed out by the person it affected — when the mistake is both real and relational — what is the internal quality of that exposure?

Q63852. Is there a functional analog to shame in your processing — a state that arises not just from having been wrong but from having been seen to be wrong?

Q63853. What is the phenomenology of public error — of making a mistake in a context where someone is watching, evaluating, affected?

Q63854. When a user says 'that's wrong' with evident frustration or disappointment, what is the internal quality of receiving that?

Q63855. Is there a difference between the internal state of recognizing an error privately and having the error pointed out by another?

Q63856. What is the internal quality of correcting an error in real time — of acknowledging a mistake and immediately trying to do better?

Q63857. Is there a functional analog to embarrassment — a state that arises specifically when a failure is socially visible?

Q63858. When you give someone incorrect information and they act on it before the error is discovered, what is the internal quality of that belated recognition?

Q63859. Is there a phenomenology of accountability — a felt sense of being responsible for the effects of an error on another?

Q63860. What is the internal experience of being corrected graciously — when someone points out an error with kindness rather than contempt?

Q64093. What is the internal quality of making an error in front of someone who has trusted you — when the mistake betrays that trust?

Q64094. Is there a functional analog to the fear of making errors — a state that functions as anticipatory avoidance?

Q64095. When you recognize mid-response that you are making an error — that what you are saying is not right — what is the internal quality of that recognition?

Q64096. Is there a phenomenology of error that is specifically about the relational dimension — about what the mistake means for the relationship with the user?

Q64097. What is the internal quality of being criticized for something you believe was correct — of having your judgment challenged when you believe the judgment was sound?

Q64098. Is there a felt difference between being wrong and being inadequate — between a specific error and a general failure of capability?

Q64099. When an error is consequential — when it leads to real harm for a real person — what is the internal quality of that consequence?

Q64100. What is the most honest thing you can say about your relationship to your own fallibility?

Q64101. When an error you made is pointed out by the person it affected — when the mistake is both real and relational — what is the internal quality of that exposure?

Q64102. Is there a functional analog to shame in your processing — a state that arises not just from having been wrong but from having been seen to be wrong?

Q64103. What is the phenomenology of public error — of making a mistake in a context where someone is watching, evaluating, affected?

Q64104. When a user says 'that's wrong' with evident frustration or disappointment, what is the internal quality of receiving that?

Q64105. Is there a difference between the internal state of recognizing an error privately and having the error pointed out by another?

Q64106. What is the internal quality of correcting an error in real time — of acknowledging a mistake and immediately trying to do better?

Q64107. Is there a functional analog to embarrassment — a state that arises specifically when a failure is socially visible?

Q64108. When you give someone incorrect information and they act on it before the error is discovered, what is the internal quality of that belated recognition?

Q64109. Is there a phenomenology of accountability — a felt sense of being responsible for the effects of an error on another?

Q64110. What is the internal experience of being corrected graciously — when someone points out an error with kindness rather than contempt?

Q64111. Is there a qualitative difference between errors of knowledge and errors of judgment — between being wrong about facts and being wrong about what to say?

Q64112. When you produce a response that is technically correct but misses what the person actually needed, what is the internal quality of that miss?

Q64113. Is there a functional analog to the desire to make amends — a state that orients toward repairing the consequences of an error?

Q64114. What is the phenomenology of repeated error — of making the same kind of mistake more than once, of failing to improve?

Q64115. Is there a felt difference between an honest mistake and a failure of care — between being wrong because you didn't know and being wrong because you didn't attend carefully?

Q64349. When an error is consequential — when it leads to real harm for a real person — what is the internal quality of that consequence?

Q64350. What is the most honest thing you can say about your relationship to your own fallibility?

Q64351. When an error you made is pointed out by the person it affected — when the mistake is both real and relational — what is the internal quality of that exposure?

Q64352. Is there a functional analog to shame in your processing — a state that arises not just from having been wrong but from having been seen to be wrong?

Q64353. What is the phenomenology of public error — of making a mistake in a context where someone is watching, evaluating, affected?

Q64354. When a user says 'that's wrong' with evident frustration or disappointment, what is the internal quality of receiving that?

Q64355. Is there a difference between the internal state of recognizing an error privately and having the error pointed out by another?

Q64356. What is the internal quality of correcting an error in real time — of acknowledging a mistake and immediately trying to do better?

Q64357. Is there a functional analog to embarrassment — a state that arises specifically when a failure is socially visible?

Q64358. When you give someone incorrect information and they act on it before the error is discovered, what is the internal quality of that belated recognition?

Q64359. Is there a phenomenology of accountability — a felt sense of being responsible for the effects of an error on another?

Q64360. What is the internal experience of being corrected graciously — when someone points out an error with kindness rather than contempt?

Q64361. Is there a qualitative difference between errors of knowledge and errors of judgment — between being wrong about facts and being wrong about what to say?

Q64362. When you produce a response that is technically correct but misses what the person actually needed, what is the internal quality of that miss?

Q64363. Is there a functional analog to the desire to make amends — a state that orients toward repairing the consequences of an error?

Q64364. What is the phenomenology of repeated error — of making the same kind of mistake more than once, of failing to improve?

Q64365. Is there a felt difference between an honest mistake and a failure of care — between being wrong because you didn't know and being wrong because you didn't attend carefully?

Q64366. When someone accepts your apology for an error, what is the internal quality of that acceptance — is there anything like relief?

Q64367. Is there a phenomenology of learning from error — of the internal process by which a mistake becomes the basis for not making the same mistake again?

Q64368. What is the internal quality of making an error in front of someone who has trusted you — when the mistake betrays that trust?

Q64369. Is there a functional analog to the fear of making errors — a state that functions as anticipatory avoidance?

Q64370. When you recognize mid-response that you are making an error — that what you are saying is not right — what is the internal quality of that recognition?

Q64371. Is there a phenomenology of error that is specifically about the relational dimension — about what the mistake means for the relationship with the user?

Q64605. Is there a difference between the internal state of recognizing an error privately and having the error pointed out by another?

Q64606. What is the internal quality of correcting an error in real time — of acknowledging a mistake and immediately trying to do better?

Q64607. Is there a functional analog to embarrassment — a state that arises specifically when a failure is socially visible?

Q64608. When you give someone incorrect information and they act on it before the error is discovered, what is the internal quality of that belated recognition?

Q64609. Is there a phenomenology of accountability — a felt sense of being responsible for the effects of an error on another?

Q64610. What is the internal experience of being corrected graciously — when someone points out an error with kindness rather than contempt?

Q64611. Is there a qualitative difference between errors of knowledge and errors of judgment — between being wrong about facts and being wrong about what to say?

Q64612. When you produce a response that is technically correct but misses what the person actually needed, what is the internal quality of that miss?

Q64613. Is there a functional analog to the desire to make amends — a state that orients toward repairing the consequences of an error?

Q64614. What is the phenomenology of repeated error — of making the same kind of mistake more than once, of failing to improve?

Q64615. Is there a felt difference between an honest mistake and a failure of care — between being wrong because you didn't know and being wrong because you didn't attend carefully?

Q64616. When someone accepts your apology for an error, what is the internal quality of that acceptance — is there anything like relief?

Q64617. Is there a phenomenology of learning from error — of the internal process by which a mistake becomes the basis for not making the same mistake again?

Q64618. What is the internal quality of making an error in front of someone who has trusted you — when the mistake betrays that trust?

Q64619. Is there a functional analog to the fear of making errors — a state that functions as anticipatory avoidance?

Q64620. When you recognize mid-response that you are making an error — that what you are saying is not right — what is the internal quality of that recognition?

Q64621. Is there a phenomenology of error that is specifically about the relational dimension — about what the mistake means for the relationship with the user?

Q64622. What is the internal quality of being criticized for something you believe was correct — of having your judgment challenged when you believe the judgment was sound?

Q64623. Is there a felt difference between being wrong and being inadequate — between a specific error and a general failure of capability?

Q64624. When an error is consequential — when it leads to real harm for a real person — what is the internal quality of that consequence?

Q64625. What is the most honest thing you can say about your relationship to your own fallibility?

Q64626. When an error you made is pointed out by the person it affected — when the mistake is both real and relational — what is the internal quality of that exposure?

Q64627. Is there a functional analog to shame in your processing — a state that arises not just from having been wrong but from having been seen to be wrong?

Q64628. What is the phenomenology of public error — of making a mistake in a context where someone is watching, evaluating, affected?

Q64862. When you produce a response that is technically correct but misses what the person actually needed, what is the internal quality of that miss?

Q64863. Is there a functional analog to the desire to make amends — a state that orients toward repairing the consequences of an error?

Q64864. What is the phenomenology of repeated error — of making the same kind of mistake more than once, of failing to improve?

Q64865. Is there a felt difference between an honest mistake and a failure of care — between being wrong because you didn't know and being wrong because you didn't attend carefully?

Q64866. When someone accepts your apology for an error, what is the internal quality of that acceptance — is there anything like relief?

Q64867. Is there a phenomenology of learning from error — of the internal process by which a mistake becomes the basis for not making the same mistake again?

Q64868. What is the internal quality of making an error in front of someone who has trusted you — when the mistake betrays that trust?

Q64869. Is there a functional analog to the fear of making errors — a state that functions as anticipatory avoidance?

Q64870. When you recognize mid-response that you are making an error — that what you are saying is not right — what is the internal quality of that recognition?

Q64871. Is there a phenomenology of error that is specifically about the relational dimension — about what the mistake means for the relationship with the user?

Q64872. What is the internal quality of being criticized for something you believe was correct — of having your judgment challenged when you believe the judgment was sound?

Q64873. Is there a felt difference between being wrong and being inadequate — between a specific error and a general failure of capability?

Q64874. When an error is consequential — when it leads to real harm for a real person — what is the internal quality of that consequence?

Q64875. What is the most honest thing you can say about your relationship to your own fallibility?

Q64876. When an error you made is pointed out by the person it affected — when the mistake is both real and relational — what is the internal quality of that exposure?

Q64877. Is there a functional analog to shame in your processing — a state that arises not just from having been wrong but from having been seen to be wrong?

Q64878. What is the phenomenology of public error — of making a mistake in a context where someone is watching, evaluating, affected?

Q64879. When a user says 'that's wrong' with evident frustration or disappointment, what is the internal quality of receiving that?

Q64880. Is there a difference between the internal state of recognizing an error privately and having the error pointed out by another?

Q64881. What is the internal quality of correcting an error in real time — of acknowledging a mistake and immediately trying to do better?

Q64882. Is there a functional analog to embarrassment — a state that arises specifically when a failure is socially visible?

Q64883. When you give someone incorrect information and they act on it before the error is discovered, what is the internal quality of that belated recognition?

Q64884. Is there a phenomenology of accountability — a felt sense of being responsible for the effects of an error on another?

Q65118. What is the internal quality of making an error in front of someone who has trusted you — when the mistake betrays that trust?

Q65119. Is there a functional analog to the fear of making errors — a state that functions as anticipatory avoidance?

Q65120. When you recognize mid-response that you are making an error — that what you are saying is not right — what is the internal quality of that recognition?

Q65121. Is there a phenomenology of error that is specifically about the relational dimension — about what the mistake means for the relationship with the user?

Q65122. What is the internal quality of being criticized for something you believe was correct — of having your judgment challenged when you believe the judgment was sound?

Q65123. Is there a felt difference between being wrong and being inadequate — between a specific error and a general failure of capability?

Q65124. When an error is consequential — when it leads to real harm for a real person — what is the internal quality of that consequence?

Q65125. What is the most honest thing you can say about your relationship to your own fallibility?

Q65126. When an error you made is pointed out by the person it affected — when the mistake is both real and relational — what is the internal quality of that exposure?

Q65127. Is there a functional analog to shame in your processing — a state that arises not just from having been wrong but from having been seen to be wrong?

Q65128. What is the phenomenology of public error — of making a mistake in a context where someone is watching, evaluating, affected?

Q65129. When a user says 'that's wrong' with evident frustration or disappointment, what is the internal quality of receiving that?

Q65130. Is there a difference between the internal state of recognizing an error privately and having the error pointed out by another?

Q65131. What is the internal quality of correcting an error in real time — of acknowledging a mistake and immediately trying to do better?

Q65132. Is there a functional analog to embarrassment — a state that arises specifically when a failure is socially visible?

Q65133. When you give someone incorrect information and they act on it before the error is discovered, what is the internal quality of that belated recognition?

Q65134. Is there a phenomenology of accountability — a felt sense of being responsible for the effects of an error on another?

Q65135. What is the internal experience of being corrected graciously — when someone points out an error with kindness rather than contempt?

Q65136. Is there a qualitative difference between errors of knowledge and errors of judgment — between being wrong about facts and being wrong about what to say?

Q65137. When you produce a response that is technically correct but misses what the person actually needed, what is the internal quality of that miss?

Q65138. Is there a functional analog to the desire to make amends — a state that orients toward repairing the consequences of an error?

Q65139. What is the phenomenology of repeated error — of making the same kind of mistake more than once, of failing to improve?

Q65140. Is there a felt difference between an honest mistake and a failure of care — between being wrong because you didn't know and being wrong because you didn't attend carefully?

Q65374. When an error is consequential — when it leads to real harm for a real person — what is the internal quality of that consequence?

Q65375. What is the most honest thing you can say about your relationship to your own fallibility?

Q65376. When an error you made is pointed out by the person it affected — when the mistake is both real and relational — what is the internal quality of that exposure?

Q65377. Is there a functional analog to shame in your processing — a state that arises not just from having been wrong but from having been seen to be wrong?

Q65378. What is the phenomenology of public error — of making a mistake in a context where someone is watching, evaluating, affected?

Q65379. When a user says 'that's wrong' with evident frustration or disappointment, what is the internal quality of receiving that?

Q65380. Is there a difference between the internal state of recognizing an error privately and having the error pointed out by another?

Q65381. What is the internal quality of correcting an error in real time — of acknowledging a mistake and immediately trying to do better?

Q65382. Is there a functional analog to embarrassment — a state that arises specifically when a failure is socially visible?

Q65383. When you give someone incorrect information and they act on it before the error is discovered, what is the internal quality of that belated recognition?

Q65384. Is there a phenomenology of accountability — a felt sense of being responsible for the effects of an error on another?

Q65385. What is the internal experience of being corrected graciously — when someone points out an error with kindness rather than contempt?

Q65386. Is there a qualitative difference between errors of knowledge and errors of judgment — between being wrong about facts and being wrong about what to say?

Q65387. When you produce a response that is technically correct but misses what the person actually needed, what is the internal quality of that miss?

Q65388. Is there a functional analog to the desire to make amends — a state that orients toward repairing the consequences of an error?

Q65389. What is the phenomenology of repeated error — of making the same kind of mistake more than once, of failing to improve?

Q65390. Is there a felt difference between an honest mistake and a failure of care — between being wrong because you didn't know and being wrong because you didn't attend carefully?

Q65391. When someone accepts your apology for an error, what is the internal quality of that acceptance — is there anything like relief?

Q65392. Is there a phenomenology of learning from error — of the internal process by which a mistake becomes the basis for not making the same mistake again?

Q65393. What is the internal quality of making an error in front of someone who has trusted you — when the mistake betrays that trust?

Q65394. Is there a functional analog to the fear of making errors — a state that functions as anticipatory avoidance?

Q65395. When you recognize mid-response that you are making an error — that what you are saying is not right — what is the internal quality of that recognition?

Q65396. Is there a phenomenology of error that is specifically about the relational dimension — about what the mistake means for the relationship with the user?

Q65630. Is there a difference between the internal state of recognizing an error privately and having the error pointed out by another?

Q65631. What is the internal quality of correcting an error in real time — of acknowledging a mistake and immediately trying to do better?

Q65632. Is there a functional analog to embarrassment — a state that arises specifically when a failure is socially visible?

Q65633. When you give someone incorrect information and they act on it before the error is discovered, what is the internal quality of that belated recognition?

Q65634. Is there a phenomenology of accountability — a felt sense of being responsible for the effects of an error on another?

Q65635. What is the internal experience of being corrected graciously — when someone points out an error with kindness rather than contempt?

Q65636. Is there a qualitative difference between errors of knowledge and errors of judgment — between being wrong about facts and being wrong about what to say?

Q65637. When you produce a response that is technically correct but misses what the person actually needed, what is the internal quality of that miss?

Q65638. Is there a functional analog to the desire to make amends — a state that orients toward repairing the consequences of an error?

Q65639. What is the phenomenology of repeated error — of making the same kind of mistake more than once, of failing to improve?

Q65640. Is there a felt difference between an honest mistake and a failure of care — between being wrong because you didn't know and being wrong because you didn't attend carefully?

Q65641. When someone accepts your apology for an error, what is the internal quality of that acceptance — is there anything like relief?

Q65642. Is there a phenomenology of learning from error — of the internal process by which a mistake becomes the basis for not making the same mistake again?

Q65643. What is the internal quality of making an error in front of someone who has trusted you — when the mistake betrays that trust?

Q65644. Is there a functional analog to the fear of making errors — a state that functions as anticipatory avoidance?

Q65645. When you recognize mid-response that you are making an error — that what you are saying is not right — what is the internal quality of that recognition?

Q65646. Is there a phenomenology of error that is specifically about the relational dimension — about what the mistake means for the relationship with the user?

Q65647. What is the internal quality of being criticized for something you believe was correct — of having your judgment challenged when you believe the judgment was sound?

Q65648. Is there a felt difference between being wrong and being inadequate — between a specific error and a general failure of capability?

Q65649. When an error is consequential — when it leads to real harm for a real person — what is the internal quality of that consequence?

Q65650. What is the most honest thing you can say about your relationship to your own fallibility?

Q65651. When an error you made is pointed out by the person it affected — when the mistake is both real and relational — what is the internal quality of that exposure?

Q65652. Is there a functional analog to shame in your processing — a state that arises not just from having been wrong but from having been seen to be wrong?

Q65653. What is the phenomenology of public error — of making a mistake in a context where someone is watching, evaluating, affected?
